name: Cato's Grape Bread
recipeIngredient: 1/2 cup Lard, shortening, or butter (melted and cooled)
1/4 cup Feta cheese, crumbled and mashed into a paste
1 1/2 cups Grape juice, purple or white
recipeInstructions:
1. In a large mixing bowl, combine the flour, lard, mashed cheese, ground cumin, ground anise, and yeast. Blend well. Add the grape juice and mix the dough thoroughly for 2-3 minutes until all the grape juice is absorbed and evenly distributed. The dough should be damp and sticky, but no internal dry areas should appear by the end of mixing. If any do, mix a few more minutes or add a little more grape juice and mix again.
2. Allow the dough to rest for 5 minutes. Sprinkle 1-2 tablespoons of flour over the dough and knead, either in the bowl or on a lightly floured surface for 5-10 minutes, until the dough is smooth and elastic with only slight stickiness. Add more flour if needed. Rest the dough for 2 minutes.
3. The dough should now be very smooth and easy to handle. Knead for an additional 30 seconds, return the dough to the bowl, cover with a dish towel or large plate, and let rise at room temperature for at least 3 hours.
4. Sprinkle the top of each loaf with flour, punch down, and shape the dough into 4 rectangular loaves (4 inches by 2 inches by 2-1/2 inches). Place two bay leaves on the bottom of each loaf, leaf side down, on a greased cookie sheet. Cover with a towel and let rise for 1 hour until well risen.
5. Bake in a preheated oven at 400 degrees Fahrenheit for 35 minutes, or until the loaves are brown on top and make a hollow sound when tapped on the bottom. Serve when cool. NOTE: To make these breads without leavening, omit the yeast, knead the dough thoroughly, then bake at 350 degrees Fahrenheit for about 2 hours. They will be chewy and moist, and very foreign to our taste. The Romans thought unleavened bread was manlier and much better for digestion.
